name: Import Existing Cloud Resources
description: Bring existing cloud resources under Terraform management without recreating or disrupting them.
trendingReason: Organizations adopting IaC need to import manually-created cloud resources into Terraform rather than rebuilding from scratch.

steps.title: Write the resource block for the existing resource
steps.description: Define a resource block in your configuration that matches the existing cloud resource's type and settings.
steps.tip: Check the provider documentation for all required and optional arguments for the resource type.

steps.title: Import the resource into state
steps.description: Run terraform import to associate the existing resource with your new resource block in state.
steps.warning: Import only updates state. If your configuration does not match the real resource, the next apply will modify it.

steps.title: Generate a plan to find config drift
steps.description: Run plan to see what differences exist between your written config and the actual resource attributes.

steps.title: Adjust configuration until plan is clean
steps.description: Update your .tf file to match the real resource attributes until terraform plan shows no changes.
steps.tip: Exit code 0 means no changes. Exit code 2 means there are still differences to resolve.

steps.title: Use import blocks for bulk import (Terraform 1.5+)
steps.description: For Terraform 1.5 and later, use declarative import blocks to import multiple resources in one apply.
steps.command:
cat <<'EOF' >> imported.tf
import {
  to = aws_s3_bucket.existing
  id = "my-existing-bucket"
}
EOF
terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf
steps.tip: The -generate-config-out flag auto-generates the resource config for you, saving manual work.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Terraform Import Script
# Imports an existing AWS resource into Terraform state

RESOURCE_TYPE="${1:?Usage: $0 <resource_type> <resource_name> <resource_id>}"
RESOURCE_NAME="${2:?Usage: $0 <resource_type> <resource_name> <resource_id>}"
RESOURCE_ID="${3:?Usage: $0 <resource_type> <resource_name> <resource_id>}"

ADDR="${RESOURCE_TYPE}.${RESOURCE_NAME}"

echo "==> Importing ${ADDR} (id: ${RESOURCE_ID})..."
terraform import "${ADDR}" "${RESOURCE_ID}"

echo "==> Checking for configuration drift..."
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode 2>&1 | tee /tmp/tf-import-plan.txt || {
  EXIT_CODE=$?
  if [ "$EXIT_CODE" -eq 2 ]; then
    echo ""
    echo "WARNING: Configuration does not match imported resource."
    echo "Review the plan output above and update your .tf files."
    echo "Run 'terraform plan' again after making changes."
  fi
}

echo ""
echo "==> Current state for imported resource:"
terraform state show "${ADDR}"

faq.question: Does terraform import change the actual resource?
faq.answer: No. Import only updates Terraform state. No changes are made to the actual infrastructure until you run terraform apply.

faq.question: Can I import resources into a module?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the full module path: terraform import module.mymodule.aws_s3_bucket.existing my-bucket-id.

faq.question: What if I do not know all the resource attributes?
faq.answer: With Terraform 1.5+, use 'terraform plan -generate-config-out=generated.tf' after adding an import block. Terraform generates the config automatically from the imported state.
